VITAL.title: Vitals
VITAL.background: Vitals are essential medical indicators that reflect the body's fundamental functions and overall health status. They help assess a person’s physical condition, detect potential health issues, and monitor recovery. Key vital signs include pulse rate, which measures the number of heartbeats per minute and indicates heart function; irregular heartbeat count, which detects abnormal heart rhythms that may signal cardiac concerns; breathing rate, which tracks the number of breaths per minute and reflects respiratory health; systolic blood pressure, which measures the pressure in arteries when the heart contracts, showing how forcefully blood is pumped; and diastolic blood pressure, which represents the pressure in arteries when the heart rests between beats, indicating baseline arterial pressure. Monitoring these vitals regularly is crucial for identifying potential health risks early and ensuring overall well-being.

list.name: Pulse Rate
list.background: Pulse Rate is the number of times the user's heart beats, expressed as a rate per minute.

list.name: Irregular Heartbeat Count
list.background:
An irregular heartbeat is one that occurs outside of the heart's normal rhythm. The beat may have occurred sooner than expected or subject's heart may have seemed to "skip" a beat. Sometimes it can feel like a thump in subject's chest, or the feeling that their heart is beating quickly or fluttering.

Irregular heartbeats are usually harmless. Most often they are triggered by things that increase adrenaline in the body like anxiety, caffeine, and nicotine.

In rare cases, they could indicate a more serious condition like an electrolyte imbalance or a heart condition. Subject should consult their doctor if they have a persistent irregular heartbeat. Subject should seek immediate medical attention if the onset of irregular heartbeats is combined with other symptoms of a heart problem like dizziness, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

list.name: Breathing Rate
list.background:
The normal resting breathing rate for adults is between 12 and 25 breaths per minute.

A slower breathing rate may be caused by certain medications or suggest an underlying medical condition, for example, hypothyroidism, heart condition, electrolyte imbalance, etc.

A faster breathing rate could have many causes including walking fast or exercise, asthma, anxiety, infection, or cardiovascular condition. Subject may want to consult their doctor if their breathing rate falls outside of the normal range.

list.name: Breathing Rate
list.background:
The normal resting breathing rate for adults is between 12 and 25 breaths per minute.

A slower breathing rate may be caused by certain medications or suggest an underlying medical condition, for example, hypothyroidism, heart condition, electrolyte imbalance, etc.

A faster breathing rate could have many causes including walking fast or exercise, asthma, anxiety, infection, or cardiovascular condition. Subject may want to consult their doctor if their breathing rate falls outside of the normal range.

list.name: Systolic Blood Pressure
list.background: Systolic blood pressure is the peak pressure in subject's brachial arteries during the contraction of their heart muscle, measured in millimetres of mercury (mmHg).

list.name: Diastolic Blood Pressure
list.background: Diastolic blood pressure is the amount of pressure in subject's brachial arteries when their heart muscle is relaxed, measured in millimetres of mercury (mmHg).

PHYSIO.title: Physiological
PHYSIO.background: Physiological metrics provide a deeper understanding of cardiovascular function, stress adaptation, and circulatory efficiency. Heart Rate Variability (HRV) measures the variation in time between heartbeats, offering insights into autonomic nervous system balance and the body's ability to respond to stress. Cardiac Workload reflects the effort exerted by the heart to pump blood, indicating cardiovascular strain and efficiency under different conditions. Vascular Capacity assesses the ability of blood vessels to expand and contract, which plays a crucial role in maintaining proper circulation and overall heart health. Regular monitoring of these metrics helps in evaluating stress levels, cardiovascular resilience, and potential risks associated with heart function.

list.name: Heart Rate Variability
list.background:
Heart Rate Variability (HRV) refers to the variability in the timing between one heartbeat and the next. One way to measure HRV is SDNN, the calculated standard deviation (in milliseconds) of observed intervals that fall between normal (NN) heartbeats. A higher SDNN corresponds to more inter-beat variability. DeepAffex estimates SDNN as HRV_SDNN.

Increased heart rate variability suggests an increase in parasympathetic activity and/or a decrease in sympathetic activity of the autonomic nervous system.

High resting heart rate variability is considered healthy and means that the heart is more flexible to the changing needs of the body.

list.name: Cardiac Workload
list.background:
Cardiac Workload, or more precisely myocardial workload, is a measure of the stress put on the heart muscle.

When measured at rest, this index can be used as an indicator of cardiovascular health.

For example, a person who exercises regularly may have a lower Cardiac Workload than another person who lives a sedentary lifestyle. During an intense physical exercise, subject's Cardiac Workload may increase but should decrease post-exercise.

list.name: Vascular Capacity
list.background:
Vascular Capacity or Tau, is a measure of the elasticity of subject's blood vessels.

When measured at rest, this index can be used as an indicator of cardiovascular health as it is strongly correlated with vascular stiffness.

A person with a high Tau has better vascular health compared to a person with a low Tau. Also, certain transient activities and physiological events can lead to immediate changes in Tau (e.g., drinking alcohol, smoking).

MENTAL.title: Mental
MENTAL.background: Mental metrics help assess cognitive and emotional well-being by analyzing physiological responses related to stress. The NuraLogix Mental Stress Index evaluates stress levels by measuring physiological patterns influenced by the autonomic nervous system. This index provides insights into how the body reacts to mental and emotional stress, helping individuals understand their stress responses and manage their overall mental health. Regular monitoring of this metric can aid in stress management, improving overall well-being and cognitive performance.

list.name: NuraLogix Mental Stress Index
list.background: Mental Stress is the change in the internal homeostasis caused by a psychological, physiological, or environmental stressor. Stimuli that cause stress result in the activation of sympathetic nervous system rendering a chain of physiological changes.

PHYSICAL.title: Physical
PHYSICAL.background: Physical metrics provide valuable insights into overall body composition, health risks, and fitness levels. Body Mass Index (BMI) is a widely used measure that evaluates body weight in relation to height, helping to categorize individuals into weight-related health risk groups. Facial Skin Age analyzes skin features to estimate biological aging, offering insights into skin health and lifestyle effects. Waist-to-Height Ratio helps assess fat distribution and cardiovascular risk by comparing waist circumference to height. Body Shape Index refines obesity-related risk assessment by considering both BMI and waist measurements. Estimated Height and Estimated Weight provide approximate values based on facial and body analysis, aiding in overall health evaluations. Waist Circumference is a key indicator of abdominal fat, which is linked to metabolic and cardiovascular health risks. Regular monitoring of these metrics helps in maintaining a healthy lifestyle and identifying potential health concerns early.

list.name: Body Mass Index
list.background:
Body Mass Index is a measure of an individual's tissue mass (muscle, fat, and bone) adjusted for height. It is a commonly used indicator of overall body fat and serves as a tool for categorizing individuals as being underweight, normal weight, overweight, or obese on the basis of health risk.

Body Mass Index is a fair predictor of cardiovascular diseases, Type 2 diabetes, gallstones, and certain cancers. It is the one of the best anthropometric predictors of high-density lipoprotein (HDL) and triglyceride levels; these are important factors for cardio-metabolic diseases. It is also one of the best anthropometric predictors of uric acid levels (high uric acid levels contribute to gallstones). A limitation of Body Mass Index is that it cannot distinguish between fat and muscle mass and thus can be inaccurate in muscular individuals.

list.name: Facial Skin Age
list.background:
This estimate can be used as an indicator for subject's facial skin aging. It may be influenced by many factors such as fatigue level, the use of skincare or cosmetics products.

Video images taken in poor lighting conditions (e.g., back-lighting, overhead lighting) may distort the estimation of subject's facial skin age.

list.name: Waist-to-Height Ratio
list.background: It is a particularly good predictor of cardiovascular disease risk. It is associated with cardiovascular disease risk factors like elevated total cholesterol and lower high-density lipoprotein (HDL).

list.name: Body Shape Index
list.background:
A low Body Shape Index corresponds to a ‘chilli pepper'-shaped body. As BSI increases, body shape resembles a pear, then a pineapple, and then an apple. A larger abdominal region suggests elevated levels of deep abdominal fat, which is associated with various health risks.

Body Shape Index is a particularly good predictor of mortality (from all causes), as well as a fair predictor of diabetes and cardiovascular diseases. It is associated with elevated triglyceride levels, which may partially contribute to the development of cardiovascular diseases.

list.name: Estimated Height
list.background: Estimated Height represents the approximate measurement of an individual's stature from head to toe. Height is a key anthropometric parameter used to assess overall growth, health status, and physical development. It can also be a factor in determining Body Mass Index (BMI) and is considered in various health risk assessments. Variations in height may be influenced by genetic factors, nutrition, and overall well-being.

list.name: Estimated Weight
list.background: Estimated Weight represents the approximate body mass of an individual. It is a fundamental parameter in health assessments, used to calculate Body Mass Index (BMI) and evaluate physical fitness. Weight fluctuations may reflect changes in muscle mass, fat percentage, or water retention, and can be influenced by factors such as diet, exercise, and overall health status. Maintaining a healthy weight is essential for reducing the risk of chronic conditions like cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and metabolic disorders.

list.name: Waist Circumference
list.background: Waist Circumference is the measurement around the narrowest part of the waist, typically just above the hip bones. It is a key indicator of abdominal fat and is used to assess the risk of metabolic conditions such as obesity, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Higher waist circumference values are associated with an increased risk of health complications, as central obesity is linked to greater visceral fat accumulation. Regular monitoring helps in managing weight-related health risks.

GENERAL.title: General Risks
GENERAL.background: General risk metrics help assess the likelihood of developing serious health conditions by analyzing key physiological and physical indicators. Cardiovascular Disease Risk evaluates the overall probability of developing heart-related conditions by considering factors such as blood pressure, heart rate variability, and vascular health. Heart Attack Risk estimates the potential for a cardiac event by analyzing stress on the heart, arterial condition, and other cardiovascular parameters. Stroke Risk assesses the likelihood of a stroke by examining factors like blood pressure, vascular capacity, and irregular heartbeat patterns. Regular monitoring of these risk indicators enables early intervention, lifestyle adjustments, and proactive health management to reduce potential complications.

list.name: Cardiovascular Disease Risk
list.background:
This risk score considers subject's biological sex at birth, age, Body Mass Index, systolic blood pressure and antihypertensive medication status, as well as their smoking status and diabetes status when available.

It was developed using a concept derived from the Framingham method. It is based on data from prospective studies that followed participants' cardiovascular health for over 10 years.

list.name: Heart Attack Risk
list.background:
This risk score considers subject's biological sex at birth, age, Body Mass Index, systolic blood pressure and antihypertensive medication status, as well as their smoking status and diabetes status when available.

It was developed using a concept derived from the Framingham method. It is based on data from prospective studies that followed participants' cardiovascular health for over 10 years.

list.name: Stroke Risk
list.background:
This risk score considers subject's biological sex at birth, age, Body Mass Index, systolic blood pressure and antihypertensive medication status, as well as their smoking status and diabetes status when available.

It was developed using a concept derived from the Framingham method. It is based on data from prospective studies that followed participants' cardiovascular health for over 10 years.

METABOLIC.title: Metabolic Risks
METABOLIC.background: Metabolic risk metrics help evaluate the likelihood of developing conditions related to metabolism, blood sugar regulation, and lipid levels. Hypertension Risk assesses the probability of high blood pressure, which can lead to cardiovascular complications if left unmanaged. Type 2 Diabetes Risk estimates the likelihood of developing insulin resistance and abnormal blood sugar levels based on physiological markers. Hypercholesterolemia Risk evaluates the potential for elevated cholesterol levels, which can increase the risk of heart disease. Hypertriglyceridemia Risk analyzes the risk of high triglyceride levels, a key factor in metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular issues. Fatty Liver Disease Risk estimates the possibility of excess fat accumulation in the liver, which can lead to liver dysfunction and long-term health complications. Overall Metabolic Health Risk provides a comprehensive assessment of an individual's metabolic state by considering multiple factors, helping in early detection and prevention of metabolic disorders. Regular monitoring of these risks supports proactive health management and lifestyle modifications.

list.name: Hypertension Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on their facial blood flow (TOI) and subject profile information.

Hypertension is a major treatable risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Therefore, those with a high hypertension risk should consider getting screened for hypertension by their doctor.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

list.name: Type 2 Diabetes Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on their facial blood flow (TOI) and subject profile information. It does not necessarily reflect the user's current blood sugar level.

Uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes can cause problems like vascular disease, heart disease, kidney damage, and nerve damage. Therefore, those with a high Type 2 Diabetes Risk should consider getting screened by their doctor.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

list.name: Hypercholesterolemia Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on their facial blood flow (TOI) and subject profile information.

Hypercholesterolemia is a treatable risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Therefore, those with a high Hypercholesterolemia Risk should talk to their doctor about having their HDL and total cholesterol levels checked with a blood test.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

list.name: Hypertriglyceridemia Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on their facial blood flow (TOI) and subject profile information.

Hypertriglyceridemia in combination with other factors is a treatable risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Therefore, those with a high Hypertriglyceridemia Risk should talk to their doctor about having their triglyceride levels checked with a blood test.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

list.name: Fatty Liver Disease Risk
list.background: Fatty liver disease is characterized by the accumulation of triglyceride lipids within the liver cells, leading to an abnormal increase in liver size. Its development involves multiple contributing factors. These include excessive alcohol consumption, which impairs liver function and promotes the retention of fatty acids; metabolic syndrome, characterized by obesity and insulin resistance, which leads to the accumulation of fatty acids in the liver; and dietary factors such as high fructose intake, which contributes to the storage of excessive fat in liver cells. These factors collectively play a role in the development and progression of fatty liver disease.

list.name: Overall Metabolic Health Risk
list.background:
The combination of the outlined risks—Hypertension Risk, Type 2 Diabetes Risk, Hypertriglyceridemia Risk, Hypercholesterolemia Risk, and Fatty Liver Disease Risk—forms a comprehensive image of an individual's metabolic health. Evaluated via facial blood flow (TOI) measurements and subject's profile data, these risks highlight the complex overlap between metabolic well-being and cardiovascular health.

The combined impact of these risks emphasizes the necessity for taking proactive steps in managing one's health. The potential emergence of high blood pressure, Type 2 diabetes, elevated triglyceride and cholesterol levels, or fatty liver disease emphasizes the significance of adopting healthier habits and seeking guidance from professionals. The combined impact of these risks has a broader influence that reaches into the area of potential heart-related problems and disorders that affect blood vessels, which can include a range of cardiovascular diseases.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in the subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. Therefore, the best estimate of the subject's overall risk is obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

BLOOD.title: Blood Biomarkers
BLOOD.background: Blood biomarker metrics help assess long-term and short-term blood sugar regulation, which is crucial for detecting and managing metabolic health risks. Hemoglobin A1C Risk evaluates the likelihood of elevated A1C levels, which reflect average blood sugar levels over the past few months and indicate the risk of prediabetes or diabetes. Fasting Blood Glucose Risk estimates the probability of high blood sugar levels in a fasting state, which can signal impaired glucose metabolism or early signs of diabetes. Monitoring these biomarkers regularly helps in the early detection of metabolic disorders and enables proactive health management through lifestyle and dietary adjustments.

list.name: Hemoglobin A1C Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on their facial blood flow (TOI) and subject profile information.

HbA1c or glycated hemoglobin is formed when glucose from the blood stream combines with hemoglobin in red blood cells. The HbA1c test is a laboratory blood test ordered by the subject's health care provider. It estimates the average blood glucose level during the past 2–3 months by measuring the amount of glycated hemoglobin A1c in a person's blood.

The higher the A1c percentage, the higher the blood glucose levels, thus helping in the diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes. There are other conditions and physiological states beyond just diabetes which may alter HbA1c values. Therefore, it is important for the subject to discuss the meaning of their readings with their health care provider and determine if further assessment is required.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.

list.name: Fasting Blood Glucose Risk
list.background:
This risk profile is based on subject's facial blood flow (TOI) and their profile information.

Fasting blood glucose (FBG) test is a laboratory blood test ordered by a health care provider, performed after 8–10 hrs of fasting. This blood test is the most accurate way to determine a blood glucose result. FBG may also be determined using over the counter glucometers. These glucometers may produce FBG results that vary in accuracy by as much as +-15%. There are other conditions and physiological states beyond just diabetes which can alter FBG results. Therefore, it is important for the subject to discuss the meaning of their readings with their health care provider and determine if further assessment is required.

Notice: Moment-to-moment fluctuations in subject's physiology mean that their risk estimates could vary to some degree from one measurement to the next. The best estimate of subject's overall risk is therefore obtained by averaging several measurements throughout the day and across several days to adequately account for this physiological variation.
